pattern: A circular array can only ever be rotated into sorted order if it is made of at most one ascending run wrapped around itself, so counting how many places the array 'drops' tells you instantly whether it is fixable and, if so, exactly how far to shift.
hints: Walk the array treating it as circular (the last element's neighbor is the first), and count how many positions have a value greater than the value right after them.
If that count is zero the array is already sorted, and if it is more than one there is no rotation that can ever fix it -- more than one drop means more than one run.
When there is exactly one drop, its position tells you exactly how far around the array the smallest element currently sits from the front, which is exactly the number of right shifts needed.

edge_inputs.description: single element, already sorted by definition
two elements already in ascending order
two elements in descending order, one shift fixes it
the drop sits at the very last wraparound edge, needing zero shifts
the drop sits at the very first position, needing the maximum possible shift count
two separate drops make sorting by rotation impossible
values are sorted but not contiguous, checking magnitude gaps do not confuse the drop count

stress.description: a random permutation is rotated by a random known amount, guaranteeing the array is always sortable this way
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 100)))
    base = sorted(rng.sample(range(1, 101), n))
    r = rng.randint(0, n - 1) if n > 1 else 0
    rotated = base[n - r:] + base[:n - r] if r else base[:]
    return [rotated]

stress.description: a fully random permutation, often unsortable by rotation alone
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 100)))
    nums = rng.sample(range(1, 101), n)
    return [nums]
